part: Two Childhoods, North and South
title: My Father Danced on the Dining Room Table
summary: Joe Mosnier of the Southern Oral History Program, with biographer John Bishop, opens the July 21, 2011 interview at the Grahamsville, New York home of Dr. Emmett and Mrs. Priscilla Bassett, and Priscilla traces her politics from a Plainfield, New Jersey school of Harold Rugg textbooks through a Republican household, a classmate whose father danced on the table when Franklin Roosevelt died in 1944, a visit to Senator Clifford P. Case, an eye-opening union member at Life Magazine, and joining the Progressive Party back at Smith.

part: Two Childhoods, North and South
title: Sixteen Miles From the Carolina Line
summary: Emmett Bassett describes growing up in Henry County, Virginia about sixteen miles from the North Carolina border, losing his father in 1935, a mother who spent half her insurance to send a half-brother to Hampton Institute and St. Paul in Lawrenceville, and half-brothers and Cleveland sisters urging him toward Lincoln University and high school in the North, despite an elementary school that rarely kept a teacher.

part: Two Childhoods, North and South
title: The Only One Who Could Run the Farm
summary: Emmett tells how his father judged him the only child able to run the farm, recalls working his parents' gardens and a brother who hated farming, and describes a cousin named Ida Dotson, schooled at Chestnut Knob near Ridgeway, who skipped him from fifth to seventh grade into a hostile class of much older boys.

part: Two Childhoods, North and South
title: He Knew More History Than Their Sons
summary: Emmett sketches the white Bassett family of the furniture industry and his grandfather, a Turner raised among the Bassett sons who slept in their house but was barred from their schools, taught math and history at home until he reportedly outknew them, made boss of a sawmill shipping lumber to Grand Rapids, Michigan, and overseeing white workers until a six-million-dollar Continental Bank loan to build a furniture factory required state approval.

part: Two Childhoods, North and South
title: Virginia Wanted No Blacks in Charge
summary: Emmett recounts that around 1900 Virginia, unwilling to let a Black man supervise the operation, forced his grandfather William Henry out, after which he became a preacher marrying couples nearly weekly, that his father was likewise pushed out at the Tillesta furniture company, and that returning from the University of Massachusetts he learned of a white woman, a cousin of his father by the Tillester wife, whom he declined to visit.

part: Two Childhoods, North and South
title: We Are Cousins
summary: Emmett recalls hearing as a high school freshman about the Scottsboro defendants, boys his own age, and a swimming hole the family dammed near the Morris place where neighbors of both races swam without suits, and tells of a white Morris neighbor, his mother's first cousin, who brought two girls to swim, was reassured the children were all first cousins with nothing to fear, and afterward thanked Emmett for letting the girls swim.

part: How They Met in Massachusetts
title: A Roadhouse That Refused to Serve Blacks
summary: Priscilla recounts the road between Smith College and Amherst and a notorious roadhouse that refused Black patrons, a picket line Smith women joined, and a lawsuit after arrests, with the couple's meeting tied to that protest, and describes serving as a witness rather than a defendant at the trial, a Progressive Party lawyer careful to put no words in her mouth.

part: How They Met in Massachusetts
title: It Was Our Right and Privilege
summary: Priscilla recalls a small enclave of Black families in white Plainfield, an eighth-grade cantata in which a Black student sang Paul Robeson's part in Ballad for Americans and Robeson himself performing at the high school, and the couple reflects on being an interracial pair, Emmett noting little hostility at the University of Massachusetts and a house mother who took it in stride, Priscilla affirming it their right to proceed.

part: Tuskegee and a Black Officer's War
title: Up at Four to Milk the Cows
summary: Emmett describes entering Tuskegee on a work-study program of some twenty-four hours a week, rising at four to milk thirteen Guernsey cows by hand to a supervisor's astonishment, earning fifteen to twenty cents an hour, and taking weekend barbecue work, including a man who cooked meat all night in a wooded trench for Tuskegee's yearly community feast and said white people had been good to him.

part: Tuskegee and a Black Officer's War
title: The Farm Back Home
summary: Emmett tells how he earned high marks, an A in biochemistry, and a standing offer of an easier job and a biochemistry major, but kept thinking of the farm his family still depended on him to run, and how he brought a Black worker off a white farm by paying two dollars a day where the man had earned seventy-five cents.

part: Tuskegee and a Black Officer's War
title: Just Like a Little Spring Chicken
topic: Ralph Ellison and George Washington Carver
summary: After a recording break, Emmett describes a National Youth Administration scholarship of about twenty-five dollars a month that freed him to study and guide visitors at the George Washington Carver place, a Labor Department writer he later realized was Ralph Ellison, and Dr. Carver, who kept no distance from his scholarship students, ran a hand over the growing boy's chin calling him a spring chicken, and died, about seventy, before Emmett could get his autograph.

part: Tuskegee and a Black Officer's War
title: Nobody Who Volunteered Came Home
summary: Emmett describes the options as the war intervened, volunteering for the Tuskegee Army Air Force at about eighteen or training as an officer at Fort Valley State or in Texas, and writing his mother, who replied that no one in their family who volunteered ever came home alive, naming a great-uncle, Willie Travis, said to look more white than Black and killed at the Alamo, a story Emmett himself holds uncertainly.

part: Tuskegee and a Black Officer's War
title: I Never Wanted to Go to Prison
summary: Emmett explains that conscientious objectors he knew went to prison, which he would not do, and recounts his Martinsville, Virginia draft board, including a judge named Whittle and dairy farmers, telling him that with such a poor physical turnout among Black men they wanted to draft him, and his retort that they deferred men who worked their own farms.

part: Tuskegee and a Black Officer's War
title: Go Sit in the Back Seat
summary: Emmett recounts entering the quartermaster corps, completing both the soldier and quartermaster courses with high grades, and assignment to Company E under Colonel Snowden, the highest-ranking Black officer in a unit largely of professors, and after a break describes being summoned to a meeting of about a thousand white people, taking a front seat, a woman asking him to move back, and his reply that they could simply send him outside, which a young white officer soon did without explanation.

part: Tuskegee and a Black Officer's War
title: Everybody Wants to Be Known
summary: Emmett says segregated service kept him at Camp Lee, Virginia in Company E among professors and college presidents, and recounts attorney Robert Ming, who argued a case before the Supreme Court and traveled with him to Fort A.P. Hill, being commissioned and sent to Europe to address mistreatment of Black soldiers in France, telling Emmett everyone there sought recognition, and a hostile second lieutenant who threatened to bust the sergeant back to private.

part: Postwar Work, North and South
title: I Could Never Train a Sharecropper
summary: After the war Emmett taught agriculture in the county at about ninety dollars a month, then confronted a plan along Route 58 toward Danville where bankers who owned thousands of acres would run a plantation school training returning Black veterans for seventy-five dollars a month and federal money, only to leave them stuck as sharecroppers after three years, which he refused to teach, a program a white professor on a Julian Bond tour later recognized.

part: Postwar Work, North and South
title: Library, Nursing, School Teacher
summary: Asked about Columbus and any early stirrings on women's status, Priscilla says they first spent a couple of years in New York where, with law school out of reach and no money for graduate school, she took a New York Public Library internship, an excellent program that nonetheless channeled her into women's work, library, nursing, and teaching, which she recalls without great regret.

part: Postwar Work, North and South
title: I Could Do Something About It
topic: A Woman on the Production Line
summary: In Columbus Priscilla left library work for far better pay at General Motors, women's work on the production line where the skilled trades were almost exclusively white men, and tells of recognizing a Black co-worker in the washroom despite a wig, learning that this woman with real Curtiss-Wright factory experience was kept on ninety days probation in the washroom while Priscilla was placed directly, an injustice that drove her to become a shop steward and convinced her she could do something about it.

part: Brown, Till, and Closed Doors
title: A Meeting at the Baptist Church
summary: Priscilla recalls being in Columbus in 1954 when the Brown decree came down and in the summer of 1955 when Emmett Till was murdered, and a Baptist-church meeting and a collection at the state capitol that raised about three thousand dollars for Till's mother, covered in The Crisis, then Emmett's own required teaching of a course before his Ohio State qualifying exam and a talented colleague, Kenneth Fox, who later went to Uppsala, Sweden.

part: Brown, Till, and Closed Doors
title: The Whole Top Would Go Off the Building
summary: Emmett recounts Chicago companies flying him in and housing him at the Palmer House for two weeks of job hunting, each saying that hiring him would blow the top off the building, and a man at Beatrice Foods who said he had nearly lost his job over hiring a Chinese technician, since the dairy and food technology jobs in the heart of the Black community were all white.

part: Science at Columbia
title: Seventeen Years of Being Illegal
summary: Emmett notes his doctorate likely made him the first person of African descent to take a Ph.D. in dairy science, that Black colleges in Florida and Georgia recruited him sight unseen but the couple could not move South under miscegenation laws until Loving v. Virginia in 1967, and describes joining Columbia University's microbiology department after Sheffield Farms, banking the organic chemistry he had taken at Ohio State while declining a chemistry Ph.D. that demanded more math.

part: Science at Columbia
title: I Don't Want That Job
topic: The FBI and a Lost Position
summary: After lunch the interview turns to a position Emmett was considered for at Ohio State; his department head Dr. Gould relayed concern not about him but about friends of Priscilla's tied to a place called Iowa House, and when Emmett refused to speak with the FBI or CIA about his associations he was told plainly he could not have the job, which he did not want, while the family faced closed segregated pools, found refuge at Lake Hope, and Priscilla turned away an FBI agent while hanging laundry.

part: Science at Columbia
title: You Had to Bring In Grants
summary: Emmett describes his Columbia salary coming off grants, requiring him, a senior colleague, and the technician to fund themselves through grant money, and a productive run of five major publications on the biosynthesis of patulin and the formation of aromatic amino acids.

part: Science at Columbia
title: That Is Their Field
summary: Emmett recounts wanting to publish full findings that tyrosine and phenylalanine came from a common precursor, his radioactive-carbon results showing tyrosine could not derive from phenylalanine, and his colleague warning that Rockefeller Institute researchers controlled the field and would block a full paper, so they published only a guarded note.

part: Science at Columbia
title: They Republished It Their Way
summary: Emmett tells how, weeks later, prize-winning Rockefeller Institute researchers put out the same common-precursor finding he had been forced to soften, publishing it as their own across three better-funded groups that had cornered the field and dismissed his lab's claims as insufficiently researched, a matter he attributes to academic rather than racial politics, noting he was only a postdoctoral researcher then.

part: Schools and the New York Movement
title: No Dilemma, Mae Mallory Said
summary: Asked how their activism unfolded in mid-1950s New York around Little Rock and Greensboro, Priscilla turns to their daughter's schooling, the chance at the Fieldston ethical-culture school in Riverdale, and her reluctance to leave the public system against Emmett's memory of a one-room segregated school, resolved when Mae Mallory, a colleague of Robert F. Williams, told her to send the child wherever she would get the best education and keep on fighting.

part: Schools and the New York Movement
title: They Filled Our Car With Sugar
summary: Priscilla and Emmett describe trying to integrate three nearby Washington Heights junior high schools by balancing enrollments so no student would travel far, a walkout, a Freedom School in a Methodist church, the Ocean Hill-Brownsville fight to keep a community-controlled school open, and Emmett's front-page Daily News appearance for picketing, after which angry people filled their car's tank with sugar and their supporters, including the principal, were fired.

part: Schools and the New York Movement
title: In Friendship and Stanley Levison
summary: Emmett recounts being recruited by his neighbor Bob Lewis into the In Friendship group planning the March on Washington, encountering Stanley Levison who advised Martin Luther King, Bayard Rustin's absence in Africa to stop the French bomb, planning handed to A. Philip Randolph at the Apollo Theater, recruiting buses toward Randolph's hundred-thousand threshold, and the couple attending both the March and an earlier Prayer Pilgrimage for Freedom where Priscilla first heard Dr. King.

part: Schools and the New York Movement
title: The Locked Apartment Door
summary: Emmett and Priscilla recount returning around 1956, after the bus boycott, to an apartment they had claimed only to find the lock changed, the landlord admitting he had been told not to rent to them, and Priscilla turns to her work on the Council on Interracial Books for Children and a library-science thesis on how rejecting a child's first language alienates them, which she declined to publish, feeling it improper to take the white privilege of doing so.

part: Schools and the New York Movement
title: They Voted Not to Allow the Meetings
summary: Priscilla recalls reconnecting in the early 1960s with the Women's International League for Peace and Freedom and a talk on war toys, resistance to meetings near Fort George Avenue, and their Mitchell-Lama cooperative, a state-subsidized middle-income development where she sat on the board that voted by majority not to allow the Upper Manhattan Committee for Equal Rights to meet there, prompting the couple to move out around 1964 before going to Sweden.

part: Schools and the New York Movement
title: A Three-Way Conversation
summary: Priscilla recalls regularly going to hear Malcolm X and a sidewalk exchange that became a three-way conversation with Michael Harrington, while Emmett says he went three or four times to Malcolm's Harlem speeches before the break with the Nation, the couple living not far from where Malcolm was assassinated and out for a walk that morning, before the talk turns to the war, the 1967 Newark rebellion during which they were in Sweden, and Emmett's two years at Ortho Pharmaceutical.

part: Industry, Sweden, and a Medical College
title: They Bought Blood Through a Front
topic: Leaving Johnson and Johnson for Newark
summary: Emmett explains he left Ortho Pharmaceutical, part of Johnson and Johnson, troubled by product testing in poor communities and blood bought through a front group rather than under the company's name, and that the New Jersey College of Medicine and Dentistry, standing in a Newark just torn by rebellion with no African Americans on its faculty, recruited him for admissions, offered free tuition for his daughter and a raise, though Black doctors meant to cover his clinical duties never showed up.

part: Industry, Sweden, and a Medical College
title: The Stationmaster Took Us In
summary: Asked about their year in Sweden, the couple describes crossing by ocean liner to Norway, visiting a Rockefeller Institute friend whose wife had been Emmett's technician, traveling by train from Trondheim to Uppsala, a stationmaster who took them home when they found no lodging, and Emmett's easy entry to a lab through Dr. Arne Tiselius, who never came in but spoke with him often.

part: Without Struggle There Is No Progress
title: He Blended All the Issues
summary: Priscilla recounts how Dr. King's Riverside Church speech drew her by blending civil rights, economics, and the war, citing Muhammad Ali's refusal to fight in Vietnam and the poverty draft, calling King an activist philosopher she respects alongside Malcolm X, whose memorial by author Manning Marable they attended, and arguing the missing element today is the mass movement that creates such leaders; the couple mark nearly sixty-two years of marriage, recall W.E.B. Du Bois's NAACP statement that it neither opposed nor supported interracial marriage but believed those who fall in love should be free, after Loving v. Virginia struck down miscegenation laws in seventeen states, their elder daughter crediting a shared moral compass, the family's interracial history including the Alamo ancestor Paul and Ned, and Emmett's New York City Human Rights Commission work monitoring neighborhood housing groups like Reena.

part: Without Struggle There Is No Progress
title: The Recording Resumes the Same Ground
summary: After a pause the recording resumes and the couple re-tell, nearly verbatim, the preceding reflection: Dr. King's Riverside Church speech blending racial, economic, and antiwar concerns, Muhammad Ali on Vietnam and the poverty draft, King as an activist philosopher, Malcolm X and Manning Marable's book, the need for a mass movement, their long marriage, Du Bois and the NAACP on interracial marriage after Loving v. Virginia, the moral-compass remark, the Paul and Ned and Alamo ancestry, and Emmett's Human Rights Commission work with neighborhood housing groups.

part: Without Struggle There Is No Progress
title: They Go to Who Has the Inside Information
topic: Jobs, the Commission, and Beatrice Foods
summary: Resuming with new material, Emmett sums up the Human Rights Commission, noting jobs go to those with inside information or political-club ties, that he never needed a city job, but that dairy technology plants set in the heart of the Black community would not hire even a Black porter, naming Beatrice Foods around 1955, where a professor's brother, both Purdue men, thought Emmett should have disclosed his race before applying, to which he replied they would see his color when he arrived.

part: Without Struggle There Is No Progress
title: You Can See That I Am
summary: The interviewer closes by invoking Frederick Douglass's line that without struggle there is no progress, and Emmett tells his final story of registering to vote just before entering the Army, sent to a field station where the registrar handed him a blank sheet of paper, advice from a well-off Black man named Jim Mullins to write his identifying facts, his omitting his race so the registrar pointed it out, his reply that one could see he was Black, and registering without problem around 1942 or 1943.
